feed_card.headline: The neurons in your cerebral cortex are as old as you are, formed before birth and never replaced for the rest of your life.

card_visual.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 56 32' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><g stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='3.5' stroke-linecap='round'><circle cx='20' cy='16' r='7'/><path d='M27 16 H47'/><path d='M15 11 L8 6 M15 21 L8 26 M13 16 H5'/></g></svg>

teasers: The cells you think with predate your memories
How Cold War fallout dates a brain cell
The one corner that keeps making new ones

sections.content: The neurons in your cerebral cortex are as old as you are, formed before birth and never replaced for the rest of your life.

content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 118 400 102' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='126' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>How long your cells last</text><line x1='40' y1='180' x2='360' y2='180' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.45' stroke-linecap='round'/><g fill='currentColor'><circle cx='58' cy='180' r='4'/><circle cx='169' cy='180' r='4'/><circle cx='282' cy='180' r='4'/></g><circle cx='350' cy='180' r='6' fill='var(--accent)'/><g font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' fill='currentColor'><text x='58' y='164' text-anchor='middle' opacity='0.85'>gut lining</text><text x='282' y='164' text-anchor='middle' opacity='0.85'>skeleton</text><text x='169' y='199' text-anchor='middle' opacity='0.85'>red blood cells</text></g><g font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' fill='currentColor' opacity='0.65'><text x='58' y='149' text-anchor='middle'>3-5 days</text><text x='282' y='149' text-anchor='middle'>~10 years</text><text x='169' y='215' text-anchor='middle'>~4 months</text></g><text x='360' y='199' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='end'>cortex neurons</text><text x='360' y='215' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='end'>your whole life</text></svg>

sections.content: There is a comforting line that gets repeated everywhere: your body replaces all its cells every seven years, so you are physically a new person again and again. The first half is roughly true. The lining of your gut is days old, your skin a few weeks, your red blood cells about four months, and your skeleton turns over across a decade. Then there is the cerebral cortex, the wrinkled outer brain you think and remember with. Those neurons are not on the schedule at all. You were born with them, and you will die with the same ones, the cells that learned to read carried unchanged from before your earliest memory.

The proof came from an unlikely place: Cold War fallout. Between the mid-1950s and 1963, above-ground nuclear tests nearly doubled the carbon-14 in the atmosphere, and after the test-ban treaty drove the bombs underground the level has fallen steadily ever since. Every living thing takes up that carbon, and when a cell divides, the carbon built into its fresh DNA is locked in at whatever the atmosphere held that year. The DNA is not rewritten afterward, so a cell carries the carbon-14 signature of its own birth like a date stamp. Measure the level in the DNA of cortical neurons and it matches the air from the year the person was born and stays there, never climbing to the levels of later decades, which is how Kirsty Spalding and Jonas Frisen showed those neurons are made once, in development, and kept for life.
sections.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 44 400 234' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='60' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>Carbon-14 in the air over time</text><line x1='52' y1='70' x2='52' y2='240' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.45' stroke-linecap='round'/><line x1='52' y1='240' x2='366' y2='240' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.45' stroke-linecap='round'/><line x1='52' y1='232' x2='362' y2='232' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5' opacity='0.3' stroke-dasharray='6 6'/><text x='58' y='228' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='12' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='start'>pre-bomb level</text><path d='M52 232 L93 219 L109 168 L119 88 L124 72 L140 96 L166 136 L207 168 L259 192 L310 208 L362 219' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><text x='124' y='64' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' text-anchor='middle'>1963 peak</text><g font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' fill='currentColor' opacity='0.65'><text x='52' y='258' text-anchor='start'>1950</text><text x='207' y='258' text-anchor='middle'>1980</text><text x='362' y='258' text-anchor='end'>2010</text></g><text x='200' y='274' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='12' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='middle'>a cell's DNA holds the level from the year it formed</text></svg>

content.body: The idea was almost absurd: use nuclear bombs to read the birthdays of brain cells. For decades nobody could tell when a human neuron was born, because you cannot watch a living brain build itself. At the Karolinska Institute in Stockholm, Jonas Frisen realized the bomb pulse had quietly solved the problem. Hundreds of atmospheric blasts had soaked the whole biosphere in extra carbon-14 and then left a sharp, dated curve as it faded, a clock written into every cell that formed during those years. Kirsty Spalding worked out how to extract enough DNA from a single cell type and measure its trace of carbon-14 cleanly. When they read the cortex, the answer was flat: the neurons matched the year of birth and no later, settling a century-old question about whether the adult brain grows new ones.

key_figures.name: Jonas Frisen
key_figures.role: Turned the bomb pulse into a cell clock
key_figures.one_line: His Karolinska lab realized Cold War carbon-14 could date when human cells were born, and used it to show cortical neurons last a lifetime.

key_figures.name: Kirsty Spalding
key_figures.one_line: She developed the method to measure carbon-14 in the DNA of a single cell type, reading the age of neurons, fat cells and heart cells.

content.image_caption: The Castle Romeo nuclear test, 1954. Hundreds of atmospheric blasts like it doubled the carbon-14 in the air, leaving a dated stamp inside every cell formed since.
content.image_attribution: United States Department of Energy, "Castle Romeo", public domain (Wikimedia Commons)

content.title: One corner of the brain keeps making new neurons
content.body: The cortex is fixed, but the rule is not absolute everywhere in the brain. The same carbon-14 method found that the hippocampus, the seahorse-shaped structure that lays down new memories, keeps minting neurons throughout adult life, on the order of 700 a day. That is a renewal of close to 1.75 percent of the relevant pool each year, with only a gentle decline as we age. So the part of the brain most tied to forming fresh memories is also the one that refuses to stop building fresh cells.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 128 400 96' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='142' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>New neurons in the adult brain</text><text x='40' y='171' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' opacity='0.85' text-anchor='start'>neocortex</text><text x='360' y='171' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.7' text-anchor='end'>none added</text><text x='40' y='205' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='start'>hippocampus</text><rect x='150' y='192' width='150' height='20' rx='10' fill='var(--accent)' fill-opacity='0.16' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='225' y='206' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>~700 a day</text></svg>

content.title: Your eye holds proteins older than almost any cell
content.body: Neurons are not the only part of you that dates to the beginning. The clear core of the eye's lens is built from crystallin proteins laid down before birth and never replaced, among the oldest molecules in the body. Because they are never refreshed, they spend a lifetime collecting tiny chemical damage, which is part of why the lens slowly stiffens until reading glasses arrive around middle age, and why it can finally cloud into a cataract. The window you see through is, in its center, as old as you are.

content.title: The permanence cuts both ways
content.body: A cortex that never swaps out its neurons is a cortex that holds a lifetime of memory and skill in stable hardware. It is also one that cannot grow back what it loses. When a stroke kills a patch of brain, or a disease like Parkinson's wears neurons away, the body has no routine to replace them, which is why such damage tends to be permanent and why regrowing neurons is one of the hardest goals in medicine. The same property that preserves you is the one that leaves the brain unable to repair itself.

sections.content: There is a quiet strangeness in this. The body you carry around is mostly weeks or months old, a fountain forever renewing itself, and yet the part of you that thinks and remembers was present from the very start. You reason with cells that were there before you had a single memory, the same neurons firing when you took your first breath and when you read this line. Whatever the self is, it has a physical thread running unbroken through a body that otherwise keeps replacing itself. The cost of that continuity is steep: the cells that carry you are the ones you can never get back.

content.question: Why are the neurons of your cerebral cortex considered as old as you are?
options: They divide more slowly than other cells
They stop aging once you reach adulthood
They regrow identically every few years
They form before or around birth and are not replaced afterward
content.explanation: Cortical neurons are generated during development and almost none are added later, so the ones you have are the ones you were born with, carried for life.

content.question: How does carbon-14 from Cold War bomb tests let scientists date a cell's birth?
options: The radiation killed off older cells, leaving only new ones
Bomb carbon makes young cells glow under a scanner
A cell locks in the atmosphere's carbon-14 level when its DNA is last copied, stamping it with a date
Carbon-14 keeps building up in a cell as it ages
content.explanation: When a cell divides, the carbon in its new DNA reflects the atmospheric carbon-14 at that moment. The sharp bomb-test spike and decline act as a dated marker, so the level in a cell's DNA reveals when it last formed.

content.question: Most of your body is much younger than you. Which of these cells lasts the shortest time?
options: The cells lining your gut, replaced every few days
Your skeleton
Your red blood cells
Your skin
content.explanation: Gut lining cells live only 3 to 5 days, among the fastest-renewing in the body. Skin takes weeks, red blood cells about four months, and the skeleton roughly a decade.

content.question: What did the carbon-14 method reveal about the adult human hippocampus?
options: It loses neurons steadily and never makes new ones
It keeps generating new neurons through life, around 700 a day
Its neurons are the oldest in the whole body
It is replaced entirely every year
content.explanation: Unlike the neocortex, the hippocampus adds roughly 700 neurons a day in adulthood, about 1.75 percent of the renewing pool each year, with only a modest decline as we age.

content.question: Why does the eye's lens hold some of the oldest material in your body?
options: It is made of hard mineral, like tooth enamel
Its core proteins are laid down before birth and never replaced
It stops growing entirely at age ten
It is sealed off from carbon completely
content.explanation: The crystallin proteins in the lens core are made in the womb and never recycled, so they gather a lifetime of damage, part of why the lens stiffens and clouds with age.

content.question: Why does the brain recover so poorly from a stroke?
options: Brain cells are too small to repair themselves
The immune system attacks the healing neurons
Lost cortical neurons are not replaced, so destroyed tissue does not grow back
The brain has no blood supply of its own
content.explanation: The same fact that lets cortical neurons last a lifetime, that they are not replaced, means the brain cannot regrow neurons killed by a stroke, so such damage is often permanent.

content.myth: Your body completely replaces itself every seven years.
content.reality: Many tissues do renew, the gut in days, skin in weeks, blood in months, but some cells, including the neurons of your cerebral cortex, are never replaced and last your whole life.

content.myth: Adults cannot grow any new brain cells at all.
content.reality: The neocortex adds no new neurons, yet the hippocampus keeps making them throughout adult life, on the order of hundreds a day.

content.body: How much the adult human brain renews is genuinely unsettled. The carbon-14 work points to a steady trickle of new neurons in the hippocampus, but other teams using different methods have struggled to find them in adults, and the field is openly split on whether meaningful neurogenesis continues past childhood. Why the cortex shuts the process down so completely, and whether it could ever be coaxed into replacing lost neurons for repair, are open and consequential questions.
items: Does the adult human hippocampus truly keep making neurons, and if so, how many and how much do they matter?
Could the brain ever be prompted to replace cortical neurons lost to injury or disease?

content.label: Bhardwaj RD, Spalding KL, Frisen J, et al. Neocortical neurogenesis in humans is restricted to development. PNAS 2006;103(33):12564-12568 (cortical neurons as old as the individual, by carbon-14 dating)
Spalding KL et al. Dynamics of Hippocampal Neurogenesis in Adult Humans. Cell 2013;153(6):1219-1227 (about 700 new neurons a day, 1.75% annual turnover)
Bomb pulse. Wikipedia (above-ground nuclear tests roughly doubled atmospheric carbon-14, peaking in 1963; carbon-14 in DNA used to date when cells formed)
Our Bodies Replace Billions of Cells Every Day. Scientific American (cell turnover rates across tissues)
Lynnerup N et al. Radiocarbon Dating of the Human Eye Lens Crystallines Reveal Proteins without Carbon Turnover throughout Life. PLOS ONE 2008;3(1):e1529
Sorrells SF et al. Human hippocampal neurogenesis drops sharply in children to undetectable levels in adults. Nature 2018;555:377-381 (the contested evidence on adult neurogenesis)
